name: Mystery
description: Investigation-centered stories driven by unanswered questions, clues, and revelations, culminating in explanation or exposure of hidden truth.

nodes.label: Mystery Promise
nodes.definition: The audience is promised a solvable puzzle — a story where a central question is posed, clues are made available, and a satisfying resolution will explain the truth through logic and revelation rather than accident or authorial fiat.
exit_conditions: Core constraints are established that fulfill this promise
failure_modes: Central question is never clearly posed
Resolution depends on information withheld from the audience
Solution arrives without sufficient buildup
signals_in_text: A crime or puzzle is introduced early that demands explanation
Protagonist is positioned as investigator or questioner
Audience is implicitly invited to solve alongside the story

nodes.label: A Central Question Must Drive the Narrative
nodes.definition: Every plot movement must be organized around answering a specific, clearly posed question — who committed the act, what actually happened, why it was done, or how it was possible. The story has no engine without this question.
exit_conditions: The central question is posed, maintained, and finally answered
typical_variants: What really happened on the night of X?
How was it possible given the locked room?
failure_modes: Question is too vague to drive investigation
Multiple competing questions dilute focus
Question is answered mid-story without a replacement of equal weight
signals_in_text: Characters voice the question explicitly
Every scene moves toward or away from the answer
The audience tracks clues against the question

nodes.label: Fair Clueing — Audience Must Have Access to the Clues
nodes.definition: All clues necessary to solve the mystery must be present in the text, discoverable by an attentive audience. The story may bury clues or disguise them, but may not withhold them entirely. The audience must be able to arrive at the solution independently.
exit_conditions: All essential clues have appeared on the page or screen before the reveal
typical_variants: Negative clue (what is absent or not said)
failure_modes: Clue is introduced only at the moment of reveal
Key information exists only in the detective's head
Solution requires knowledge the audience was never given
signals_in_text: Investigator draws attention to seemingly minor details
Objects or statements are described with unusual specificity
Reader/viewer can mentally re-examine earlier scenes after the reveal

nodes.label: Red Herrings Allowed, Cheating Is Not — Solution Must Be Logically Derivable
nodes.definition: Misdirection is a legitimate tool — suspects, false trails, and misleading evidence are permitted. But the final solution must be logically derivable from the clues given. The story may deceive but not lie; it may distract but not falsify the record.
exit_conditions: Resolution is retrospectively provable using only evidence presented before the reveal
typical_variants: Suspect whose behavior is suspicious for unrelated reasons
Alibi that is technically true but misleading
failure_modes: Solution requires accepting that a prior scene misrepresented objective fact
Culprit is someone introduced only in the final chapter
Solution relies on coincidence rather than logic
signals_in_text: Red herrings are traceable to a non-criminal cause upon re-read
The culprit has been present and active throughout
Every element of the solution was knowable in advance

nodes.label: Resolution Must Explain, Not Just Reveal — the Answer Must Satisfy
nodes.definition: Naming the culprit or stating the answer is not enough. The resolution must walk through the logic — how the crime was committed, why each clue points where it does, and why all alternatives are excluded. Explanation, not just revelation, is the promised payoff.
entry_conditions: Central question has been pursued through the narrative
exit_conditions: Resolution accounts for all major clues and explains the mechanics and motive of the mystery
failure_modes: Culprit confesses without explanation of method or motive
Audience is told who but not how or why
Unexplained clues remain dangling after the reveal
signals_in_text: Investigator re-traces the logic step by step
Earlier scenes are explicitly re-contextualized
All suspects are cleared or implicated with reasoning

nodes.label: Cozy Mystery
nodes.definition: Mystery set in a small, tight-knit community featuring low violence, minimal graphic content, and an amateur sleuth whose social position within the community gives them investigative access. The tone is warm, witty, and puzzle-forward.
entry_conditions: Setting is a closed community with established social relationships
Protagonist is an amateur with community ties rather than institutional authority
exit_conditions: Community and relationships are restored or clarified by the resolution
typical_variants: Amateur sleuth with a quirky specialty
failure_modes: Violence becomes graphic and disturbing
Community is irrelevant to the investigation
Amateur sleuth solves through luck rather than observation
signals_in_text: Domestic or community settings dominate
Protagonist is charming and socially integrated
Humor and warmth coexist with the central crime
Suspects are neighbors, friends, or regulars

nodes.label: Hardboiled Mystery
nodes.definition: Mystery set in a gritty urban world featuring a morally ambiguous professional detective — typically a private investigator — who operates outside institutional authority and navigates corruption, violence, and moral compromise to reach a truth that may not redeem anyone.
entry_conditions: Setting is gritty, urban, and morally compromised
Protagonist is a professional with hard-won experience and cynical worldview
exit_conditions: Truth is exposed even if justice cannot fully be served
Detective's moral code is tested but maintained
failure_modes: Cynicism becomes nihilism with no moral code
Grittiness is aesthetic only without thematic weight
Resolution is clean and optimistic in a way that contradicts the world established
signals_in_text: First-person narration with sardonic voice
Urban squalor and institutional corruption
Protagonist operates alone and is threatened or compromised
Violence and moral ambiguity are present without glamorization

nodes.label: Police Procedural
nodes.definition: Mystery organized around institutional investigation — police, forensic, or legal processes — where authenticity of method matters as much as the puzzle. The investigation follows real-world (or realistically extrapolated) protocols and the team dynamic is as important as the individual detective.
entry_conditions: Protagonist operates within an institutional structure
Investigation follows documented or realistic procedural methods
exit_conditions: Solution is reached through the accumulated application of process, forensic evidence, and institutional cooperation
failure_modes: Procedure is ignored whenever inconvenient to the plot
Institutional context is window dressing only
Team dynamic is irrelevant to the investigation
signals_in_text: Specific investigative techniques are named and demonstrated
Chain of evidence and protocol are referenced
Team members have distinct roles and specializations
Bureaucratic and legal constraints shape what is possible

nodes.label: Locked Room / Impossible Crime
nodes.definition: Mystery where the central puzzle is an apparently impossible crime — committed in a sealed space, with no apparent means of entry or exit, or under conditions that seem to rule out any solution. The how-was-it-done question is primary; plot and character serve the puzzle.
entry_conditions: The mechanics of the crime appear to violate physical possibility
Puzzle construction is the first structural priority
exit_conditions: The impossible is revealed to be possible through a specific, non-supernatural mechanism that was always present in the world of the story
typical_variants: Death with no apparent cause or means
Crime committed in full view with no apparent opportunity
failure_modes: Solution relies on supernatural or coincidental mechanism
The impossibility is never clearly established so the reveal carries no weight
Physical mechanism is so obscure it cannot be fairly anticipated
signals_in_text: Investigator explicitly names and describes the impossible conditions
Physical environment is mapped in unusual detail
Multiple failed solutions are proposed and eliminated before the true one

nodes.label: Closed Circle of Suspects — Limited Pool of Possible Answers
nodes.definition: The story world must establish a finite, knowable set of suspects or possible solutions. The audience must be able to hold all candidates in mind simultaneously, so that the investigation is a process of elimination and the solution is genuinely satisfying.
exit_conditions: All suspects or possible answers are introduced before the investigation concludes
typical_variants: Country house or ship with no exits
Workplace or community with limited membership
Time-bounded event where only certain people were present
Logical closed set of possible mechanisms
failure_modes: New suspect introduced only at the reveal
Pool is so large no audience member could track it
Culprit is an unknown outsider with no prior presence
signals_in_text: Early scene establishes who was present or who had access
Investigator explicitly maps the suspect field
Each suspect is given motive and opportunity

nodes.label: Evidence Is Physically Present in the Story World — Discoverable
nodes.definition: The clues that solve the mystery must exist as physical or observable facts in the story world — objects, statements, behaviors, absences — not as private thoughts or memories that were never rendered. The world must be constructed so that a careful investigator could find what is there.
exit_conditions: All key clues exist as rendered facts in the text prior to the reveal
typical_variants: Behavioral anomaly observable by the audience
failure_modes: Key clue exists only in the culprit's memory revealed at the end
Evidence is described but not rendered in a way the audience could notice
Physical clue is introduced at the reveal scene for the first time
signals_in_text: Descriptive passages include specific physical details
Investigator handles or examines objects carefully
Dialogue contains information that can be evaluated for truthfulness

nodes.label: Social Dynamics Conceal and Reveal — Relationships Determine Who Lies and Why
nodes.definition: The reason characters lie, conceal, mislead, or reveal in a mystery is always rooted in their relationships — love, loyalty, fear, shame, greed, or self-protection. The social world of the story is not backdrop but mechanism: understanding who people are to each other explains why the truth is hidden.
exit_conditions: Every character's concealment or disclosure is explicable through their relational position
failure_modes: Characters lie for no discernible relational reason
Social world is too thin to support multiple motivations
All concealment is attributable to guilt rather than other relational pressures
signals_in_text: Exposition establishes who owes what to whom
Investigator probes relationships as much as events
Reveals recontextualize relationships, not just facts

nodes.label: Discovery of the Crime/Mystery Scene — the Question Is Posed
nodes.definition: A mandatory early scene in which the mystery is encountered and the central question is formally posed — a body is discovered, a disappearance is reported, an impossibility is witnessed. This scene establishes the contract with the audience and sets the investigation in motion.
exit_conditions: Central question is clearly posed
Audience understands what must be investigated and why it matters
typical_variants: Body discovered in a locked room
Victim approaches investigator with an inexplicable threat that is then fulfilled
Investigator stumbles onto a crime in progress or aftermath
failure_modes: Mystery is too vague to orient the investigation
Scene lacks weight — audience does not feel the importance of the question
Relevant characters and settings are not yet established so the discovery floats free
signals_in_text: Scene is given structural prominence — often chapter-opening or Act One climax
Investigator or surrogate character voices the question
Physical scene of the crime is described in detail sufficient to support later clue-retrieval

nodes.label: Investigation Pivot Scene — a Clue Reframes Everything
nodes.definition: A mid-story scene in which a newly discovered clue or revelation fundamentally reframes the investigation — eliminating a front-runner suspect, expanding the scope of the crime, revealing a connection not previously visible, or suggesting the question itself was wrong. The investigation must restart from a new position.
entry_conditions: Audience is following a working hypothesis
exit_conditions: Prior hypothesis is overturned or significantly complicated
Investigation must now proceed in a new direction
Audience re-evaluates earlier clues
typical_variants: The prime suspect has a confirmed alibi
A second victim is discovered who changes the pattern
The crime is revealed to be something other than what it appeared
An earlier clue is reinterpreted in light of new information
failure_modes: Pivot is predictable — audience already suspected the reframe
Pivot is arbitrary — no prior clue prepared for it
Pivot occurs but investigation continues on the same path anyway
signals_in_text: Investigator explicitly states that everything must be re-examined
Scene pauses the forward momentum for recalculation
Audience is shown the reinterpretation of an earlier clue in real time

nodes.label: Reveal/Confrontation Scene — the Solution Is Exposed
nodes.definition: The climactic scene in which the investigator names the solution, demonstrates the logic, and exposes the truth to all relevant parties — and, through them, to the audience. The scene must explain, not merely announce; it is the earned payoff of the entire investigative structure.
entry_conditions: All essential clues have been gathered
Investigator has arrived at a complete and provable theory
All primary suspects or candidates are present or accountable
exit_conditions: Central question is definitively answered with supporting logic
Culprit or solution is identified and the mechanism is explained
All significant red herrings are accounted for
typical_variants: Formal presentation of evidence to authority
Written or recorded explanation delivered to the audience
failure_modes: Reveal names the solution without explaining the logic
Clues used in the reveal were not fairly available to the audience
Culprit denies and is simply overruled without proof
Unexplained clues or suspects remain unaddressed
signals_in_text: Investigator speaks at length, re-tracing the investigation
Earlier scenes are explicitly recalled and recontextualized
Culprit's behavior, opportunity, and motive are all accounted for

nodes.label: Intellectual Engagement and Mounting Curiosity
nodes.definition: Mystery requires a sustained tone of intellectual engagement — the audience must feel the pleasure of puzzle-solving, the itch of unanswered questions, and the growing urgency of needing to know. This is curiosity as narrative engine, distinct from fear or action-excitement.
exit_conditions: Tone sustains the audience's investment in the question across the full length of the story
typical_variants: Atmospheric, menacing tone with puzzle at center (noir mystery)
failure_modes: Story substitutes action excitement for intellectual curiosity
Audience stops caring about the question because characters do not model genuine investigative investment
Pacing is so slow curiosity collapses into impatience
signals_in_text: Investigator's enthusiasm for the puzzle is visible and contagious
Clue-discovery scenes are given weight and savored
Scene-endings frequently pose new questions rather than providing answers

nodes.label: Withheld Clues / Unfair Resolution
nodes.definition: Mystery fails when the resolution depends on information that was not made available to the audience — the detective knew something the reader never could, the solution is logically underdetermined by the clues given, or the culprit is only introduced at the reveal. This violates the genre's foundational contract of fair play.
exit_conditions: Resolution uses only clues that were fairly presented — this node should not be reached
typical_variants: Key clue exists only in detective's unshared internal knowledge
Solution requires a coincidence not prepared for by the narrative
Retrospective clue invented to justify a pre-determined culprit
failure_modes: This node IS the failure mode — its presence indicates a genre contract violation
signals_in_text: Audience cannot reconstruct the solution from material presented before the reveal
Investigator says 'I knew all along' without the narrative having shown it
Resolution requires accepting facts that contradict prior scene-level presentation

edges.label: Promise requires a driving central question
edges.effects_on_stakes: Every scene must be measurable against the question — does this move us closer to or further from the answer?
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist is defined by their relationship to the question — as investigator, witness, or person with a stake in the truth
common_alternatives: Single question (whodunit) vs. layered questions (who, why, and how simultaneously)
anti_patterns: Story proceeds without a clearly posed question — events accumulate but nothing is being solved

edges.label: Promise requires clues accessible to the audience
edges.effects_on_stakes: The audience's ability to participate in the puzzle is the genre's core pleasure — withholding clues eliminates that pleasure
edges.effects_on_character: Investigator must discover and share clues in a way that allows the audience to track alongside
common_alternatives: Dramatic irony (audience has clues character lacks) vs. shared investigation (both discover together)
anti_patterns: Clues exist only in the detective's head and are revealed only at the resolution

edges.label: Promise requires logically derivable solution
edges.effects_on_stakes: The audience's trust in the story depends on knowing that the answer is findable — cheating destroys retrospective satisfaction
edges.effects_on_character: Culprit must be present and active throughout the story for the constraint to be satisfiable
common_alternatives: Misdirection through character behavior vs. misdirection through authorial framing
anti_patterns: Red herrings that rely on objective narrative falsehood rather than character deception

edges.label: Promise requires explanation, not just revelation
edges.effects_on_stakes: The payoff of the entire story depends on the resolution's completeness — an unexplained reveal is a broken promise
edges.effects_on_character: Investigator must be capable of and committed to full explanation, not just accusation
common_alternatives: Formal assembly scene vs. intimate confrontation vs. documentary reconstruction
anti_patterns: Culprit confesses without explanation of method
Resolution names who but not how or why

edges.label: Promise requires tone of intellectual curiosity
edges.effects_on_stakes: Audience investment is maintained through curiosity rather than fear or action — the tone must sustain the puzzle's appeal
edges.effects_on_character: Investigator's intellectual engagement must be visible and modeled as pleasurable, not merely mechanical
common_alternatives: Warm cozy curiosity vs. cold analytical precision vs. atmospheric dread-with-puzzle
anti_patterns: Action sequences substitute for intellectual investigation — the mystery becomes an adventure story

edges.label: Central question embedded in tight community
preconditions: Setting is a small, closed community with warm social texture
edges.effects_on_stakes: Stakes are relational and communal — the crime disrupts a social world the audience is invited to like
edges.effects_on_character: Amateur sleuth solves through social observation and community access rather than authority or force
anti_patterns: Cozy setting with graphic violence or nihilistic resolution that destroys community warmth

edges.label: Central question pursued through moral compromise and corruption
preconditions: Setting is gritty and institutionally corrupt
Protagonist is a professional operating without institutional support
edges.effects_on_stakes: Stakes include the detective's own moral integrity alongside the truth of the case
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist's worldview and code are tested by the investigation itself
anti_patterns: Hardboiled aesthetic without moral weight — grit as costume only

edges.label: Central question answered through institutional process
preconditions: Protagonist operates within a law enforcement or forensic institution
edges.effects_on_stakes: Institutional constraints (chain of evidence, warrants, protocol) are genuine obstacles and tools, not just background
edges.effects_on_character: Team dynamic and individual specializations are as important as any single investigator's insight
anti_patterns: Procedure is invoked when convenient and ignored when inconvenient

edges.label: Central question is how, not just who
preconditions: The mechanism of the crime is physically impossible as initially presented
edges.effects_on_stakes: The puzzle's integrity is the primary stake — the how-was-it-done must be answerable within the physical rules of the story world
edges.effects_on_character: Investigator must be supremely analytical, willing to eliminate every apparent solution before finding the real one
anti_patterns: Impossible crime is resolved by supernatural explanation or narrative coincidence

edges.label: Cozy community is a natural closed circle
edges.effects_on_stakes: The limited, knowable community makes the closed circle feel organic rather than contrived
edges.effects_on_character: Amateur sleuth's community membership gives access to all suspects without requiring institutional authority
anti_patterns: Cozy set in an open environment where an unknown outsider is the culprit

edges.label: Hardboiled world is driven by relational power and corruption
edges.effects_on_stakes: Power relationships — money, sex, violence, loyalty — determine who lies and who kills
edges.effects_on_character: Detective must understand the social ecology of corruption to navigate it
anti_patterns: Hardboiled world where characters' motivations are transparent and uncomplicated by relationship

edges.label: Procedural investigation is grounded in physical evidence
edges.effects_on_stakes: The case lives or dies on physical evidence — what can be proven, not just known
edges.effects_on_character: Investigators are bound by evidentiary standards; intuition must be supported by tangible proof
common_alternatives: Witness testimony corroborated by physical facts
anti_patterns: Procedural where investigators act on intuition without gathering or presenting evidence

edges.label: Impossible crime is solved through physical fact
edges.effects_on_stakes: The physical impossibility must be resolved by physical fact — there is a mechanism that was there all along
edges.effects_on_character: Investigator must be willing to observe and re-observe the physical environment until the hidden mechanism is discovered
anti_patterns: Impossible crime resolved by a mechanism not physically present in the story world

edges.label: Closed circle is the context in which the crime is discovered
preconditions: Closed circle of suspects established before or concurrent with discovery
edges.effects_on_stakes: Discovery within a closed circle immediately focuses suspicion — everyone present is a candidate
edges.effects_on_character: Investigator and audience begin tracking the closed set of suspects from the moment of discovery
common_alternatives: Discovery followed by establishment of who was present
Circle established before discovery (country house gathering)
anti_patterns: Discovery occurs in open environment with no defined suspect pool

edges.label: Physical evidence drives the investigation pivot
preconditions: Physical evidence has been collected and interpreted
edges.effects_on_stakes: A piece of physical evidence overturns the working hypothesis and re-orients the investigation
edges.effects_on_character: Investigator must be willing to abandon prior conclusions when evidence contradicts them
anti_patterns: Investigation pivots on intuition or coincidence rather than evidence

edges.label: Relational revelation reframes the investigation
preconditions: Investigation is following an initial social hypothesis
edges.effects_on_stakes: A hidden relationship — an affair, a debt, a secret alliance — overturns who had motive or opportunity
edges.effects_on_character: Investigator must probe beneath social surfaces to find the relational truth that changes everything
anti_patterns: Relational revelation that was not planted in prior characterization

edges.label: Investigation pivot enables the final reveal
preconditions: Investigation pivot has occurred and investigation has reached a new, complete theory
edges.effects_on_stakes: The pivot provided the final piece that makes the reveal possible — without it the reveal could not be complete
edges.effects_on_character: Investigator arrives at the reveal scene armed with the full picture, including what the pivot revealed
common_alternatives: Single pivot leading directly to reveal
Multiple pivots building progressively to reveal
anti_patterns: Reveal occurs without a prior pivot — investigator knew all along and withheld the knowledge from the audience

edges.label: Discovery initiates investigation that must encounter a pivot
preconditions: Discovery scene has posed the central question and investigation has proceeded
edges.effects_on_stakes: The straight path from discovery to solution is always interrupted — the pivot is what separates mystery from mere puzzle
edges.effects_on_character: Investigator who proceeds in a straight line to the solution is less interesting than one whose assumptions are overturned
anti_patterns: Investigation proceeds from discovery to reveal without significant reorientation

edges.label: Reveal scene must meet the standard of satisfying resolution
edges.effects_on_stakes: The reveal is only as good as its explanation — naming without proving is a resolution failure
edges.effects_on_character: Investigator's competence and credibility depend on the completeness and clarity of the explanation
common_alternatives: Private confrontation where culprit provides the explanation
anti_patterns: Reveal that names a culprit who cannot or does not explain themselves, leaving the logic gap for the audience to fill

edges.label: Withheld clues violate the mystery contract
preconditions: Resolution depends on information not presented to the audience before the reveal
edges.effects_on_stakes: Audience trust collapses — retrospective re-examination of the story reveals a cheat, not a puzzle
edges.effects_on_character: Investigator's intelligence is false — they solved a puzzle the audience was not permitted to solve
anti_patterns: This edge IS the anti-pattern — it describes the genre violation to avoid at all costs
